turns.content: I'm struggling with the difference between a regular Vec and the fixed-capacity Vec in heapless. I thought I could just use my standard library collections everywhere on embedded.

That is a common misconception when moving back to Rust after a break; you cannot just swap in your normal collections for no_std environments because they rely on the heap which doesn't exist there. The regular Vec requires an allocator and global memory management, whereas fixed-capacity collections like FixedVec or String from heapless are designed to work without any external allocation by strictly limiting how much space is available for them. Think of a standard Vec as a flexible bucket you can keep adding items to forever until you run out of RAM, while the heapless versions are more like a fixed-size array in memory where once it's full, nothing else fits.
Ah okay so if I need dynamic sizing then I'm stuck? Does heapless offer any way to grow?

Correct, the core philosophy of these crates is guaranteed no-alloc, meaning they will never ask for more memory than you explicitly gave them at compile time. If your application logic requires dynamic growth beyond a known maximum limit, then you simply cannot use these fixed-capacity types and must reconsider your design or implement manual buffer management instead.
Understood, so I should just pick a size that fits my worst-case scenario for the HashMap?

Exactly. For an embedded system with limited RAM like 8KB or less, you calculate the maximum number of entries your application will ever need and set that as the capacity when instantiating the FixedHashMap from heapless.
That makes sense. One last thing though, how do I handle the trait bounds since these types don't have standard methods?

The fixed-capacity collections implement the same core traits as their heap counterparts but with stricter constraints on the lifetime and capacity parameters. You just need to ensure your type is generic over the correct bounds for no_std environments.
